plainMeaning: For that very reason that city came to be known among people as Ahicchatra ("serpent-canopy"), and there we bow to Pārśva, who destroys all obstacles and is the home of every virtue.
recitation: taṃ heuṃ sā nayarī, ahicchattā nāmao jaṇe jāyā | tahiyaṃ namimo pāsaṃ, viggha-viṇāsaṃ guṇāvāsaṃ ||

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. tat (accusative tam), here adverbial with heuṃ → 'for that cause'
wordByWord.notes: Pairs with heuṃ as the phrase taṃ heuṃ = 'for that reason', pointing back to the canopy-miracle of the preceding verse.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. hetu (cause), Prakrit heu with accusative -ṃ
Skt. sā, feminine demonstrative agreeing with nayarī
Skt. nagarī (feminine of nagara), Prakrit nayarī with intervocalic g → y
ahi ('serpent') + chatta ('canopy', Skt. chattra); Skt. Ahicchatrā
wordByWord.notes: An aetiological proper name: the serpent (ahi) Dharaṇendra's raised hood became the canopy (chatta) over Pārśva.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. nāmataḥ ('by name'), Prakrit nāmao with -taḥ → -o
Skt. jane, locative of jana ('people, folk'); Prakrit n → ṇ
Skt. jātā, feminine past participle of √jan ('to be born, arise'), agreeing with nayarī
√nam ('to bow'); 1st-person plural present, Prakrit -imo ending = Skt. namāmaḥ
wordByWord.notes: The hymn's recurring homage-verb here in the inclusive plural 'we bow'.

wordByWord.gloss: Pārśva (the 23rd Tīrthaṅkara), accusative object of 'we bow'
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. Pārśva, Prakrit Pāsa with accusative -ṃ
Skt. vighna ('obstacle'), Prakrit viggha (gh-n assimilated to ggh)
wordByWord.notes: First member of the compound epithet viggha-viṇāsaṃ.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. vināśa ('destruction'), Prakrit viṇāsa; in viggha-viṇāsaṃ = 'obstacle-destroying', an accusative epithet of Pārśva
guṇa ('virtue, quality') + āvāsa ('dwelling, abode'); Skt. guṇāvāsa, accusative epithet of Pārśva

literalTranslation: For that reason that city came to be known among people by the name Ahicchatra; there we bow to Pārśva, the destroyer of obstacles, the abode of virtues.
idiomaticTranslation: And so it was that this city won its name among the people — Ahicchatra, the city of the serpent-canopy. There we bow low to Lord Pārśva, who shatters every obstacle and in whom all virtues make their home.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse is the hinge of the Ahicchatra episode: it supplies the place-name's aetiology and seals it with an act of homage. The preceding verse described Dharaṇendra rearing up to hold a threefold canopy over the meditating Pārśva at Kāśī; this verse draws the explicit consequence — taṃ heuṃ, 'for that very reason' — that the city became known as Ahicchatra, literally 'serpent-canopy' (ahi + chatta). The poet is doing folk-etymology in the classic tīrtha-māhātmya manner: the holy site's name is read as a permanent memorial of the miracle enacted there. The following verse will return to the cause of that miracle — Kamaṭha's assault with lion, elephant and demons amid an unbroken downpour — so verse 60 sits deliberately between the canopy raised and the storm explained, marking the moment the legend crystallises into a name and a shrine. The second line turns from narration to worship: tahiyaṃ namimo pāsaṃ, 'there we bow to Pārśva', the hymn's signature gesture of stringing one more tīrtha-bead onto the garland. Two accusative epithets close the verse and characterise the Jina precisely as this legend portrays him. Viggha-viṇāsaṃ, 'destroyer of obstacles', is pointed: the one who himself endured and overcame the supreme upasarga (the demon-storm) is fittingly invoked as the remover of every devotee's hindrance. Guṇāvāsaṃ, 'abode of virtues', balances the active epithet with a contemplative one — Pārśva is not merely a remover of evils but the dwelling-place of all good qualities. The verse thus compresses geography, legend and devotion into a single breath.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Jain teaching of upasarga-sahana — the equanimous endurance of affliction. Pārśva's stillness under Kamaṭha's onslaught, while a hostile being (the storm) and a devoted one (Dharaṇendra) both act around him, dramatises vītarāgatā: the Jina neither resents the attacker nor clings to the protector. The epithet viggha-viṇāsa should be read in that light — Pārśva removes obstacles not as a boon-granting deity but as the exemplar whose conquest of the deepest inner hindrances (karma, passion) empowers the reciter's own path; the devotional 'obstacle-removal' is a fruit of taking refuge in such a conqueror. Guṇāvāsa locates the proper object of devotion in the Jina's infinite qualities (ananta-guṇa) rather than in worldly favour, keeping the bow firmly oriented to liberation.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this verse, the worshipper makes a 'pilgrimage in words' to Ahicchatra without leaving the prayer-hall, recalling that Pārśva remained unmoved under deliberate torment. The practical lesson is to meet one's own 'storms' — provocations, fears, distractions in practice — with that same steadiness, and to bow to the obstacle-destroyer as a model of inner stillness rather than merely asking for trouble-free circumstances.

sources.title: Ahichhatra — historical and Jain-tīrtha identification
sources.consultedFor: The ahi+chatra etymology of the place-name and its association with Pārśvanātha and the serpent-canopy legend

notes: The verse is an aetiological naming-verse: it derives 'Ahicchatra' from ahi ('serpent') + chatta ('canopy'), commemorating Dharaṇendra's hood-canopy of the previous verse — a traditional folk-etymology, not a historical-linguistic one (the city Ahicchatrā near Bareilly, U.P., is independently attested). viggha-viṇāsaṃ and guṇāvāsaṃ are read as two accusative epithets of pāsaṃ; some editions may join or punctuate the compound viggha-viṇāsaṃ differently, but the sense ('destroyer of obstacles') is stable. jaṇe is taken as locative jane ('among people'); a reading as instrumental/agentive ('made [so named] by people') is possible but does not change the overall meaning. Orthography (e.g. અહિચ્છત્તા, વિગ્ઘ) is preserved verbatim from the source script and not normalised.
